title: The Rider on the White Horse

heb.paragraph: Hallēlouia is a Greek transliteration of Hebrew hallĕlû Yāh (praise Yah[weh]). Remarkably, this is the only occurrence of the word in the entire New Testament, yet it appears four times in this chapter (vv. 1, 3, 4, 6). The term is deeply rooted in the Psalter, where it opens and closes the Hallel psalms (Pss 113–118, 146–150). Its concentration here signals that the heavenly response to Babylon's fall is fundamentally liturgical: the appropriate reaction to divine judgment is not silence or horror but doxological praise.

refs.note: The Hallel psalms open with 'Praise the LORD' (hallĕlû Yāh)—the same cry transliterated as Hallēlouia in Revelation 19. The Hallel was sung at Passover, connecting Israel's foundational deliverance (Exodus) with the eschatological deliverance celebrated here. As Israel praised God for liberation from Egypt, the heavenly multitude praises God for liberation from Babylon.

The martyrs under the altar cried, 'How long, Sovereign Lord, holy and true, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood?' The fourfold Hallelujah of chapter 19 is the definitive answer: God has judged, God has avenged, and the heavenly host celebrates the fulfillment of the martyrs' prayer.

Moses' Song commands, 'Rejoice, you nations, with his people, for he will avenge the blood of his servants.' The heavenly rejoicing of Revelation 19:1–5 fulfills this Deuteronomic prophecy: God has avenged the blood of His servants at Babylon's hand, and heaven rejoices in covenant vindication.

notes.note: The great multitude in heaven shouts 'Hallelujah!' because 'true and just are his judgments.' This is not cruelty but worship—the celebration of divine justice finally and fully executed. God has judged the great prostitute who corrupted the earth with her adulteries and has avenged the blood of His servants. The long delay of divine justice (6:10) has ended; the martyrs' cry has been answered.

The statement 'her smoke goes up for ever and ever' describes the permanent, irreversible nature of Babylon's destruction. This is not annihilation but eternal ruin—the smoke of her burning is a perpetual testimony to God's judgment. The allusion to Isaiah 34:10 (Edom's destruction) and Genesis 19:28 (Sodom's smoke) places Babylon in the tradition of cities destroyed for their wickedness.

The twenty-four elders and four living creatures respond with 'Amen, Hallelujah!'—the only time these two liturgical responses appear together in Scripture. The voice from the throne then calls all God's servants, 'both great and small,' to praise Him. The scope is total: the entire covenant community, from the least to the greatest, participates in the eschatological Hallel.

Calvin would emphasize that the Hallelujah following Babylon's destruction is not vindictive celebration but the recognition that God's justice is a proper object of worship. When evil is destroyed, the goodness and holiness of God are displayed—and displayed goodness demands praise. The church does not rejoice in suffering but in the removal of the system that caused suffering. The permanent nature of the judgment ('her smoke goes up for ever and ever') assures the elect that the threat of Babylonian corruption is ended once for all.

The 'Amen' of the elders and living creatures confirms the multitude's praise, establishing a liturgical antiphony between the congregation and the heavenly court. Calvin saw in this pattern the basis for responsive worship in the church—the dialogue between God's revelation and the people's response. The inclusion of 'small and great' (v. 5) democratizes worship: rank and status dissolve in the presence of the God who judges righteously.

The phrase hē sōtēria kai hē doxa kai hē dynamis (salvation and glory and power) echoes 7:10 and 12:10 but adds doxa (glory), creating a trinitarian-like formula of divine attributes. The articular construction (with definite articles before each noun) indicates that these are essential, not incidental, qualities of God being celebrated.

The perfect tense anabainei (goes up) in ho kapnos autēs anabainei eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn (her smoke goes up forever and ever) has a present-tense force in Greek, indicating ongoing, continuous ascent. The phrase eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn (to the ages of the ages) is the strongest expression of eternal duration in Greek, used elsewhere for God's own eternity (4:9–10) and the torment of the beast-worshippers (14:11).

Beale identifies the fourfold Hallelujah as the eschatological fulfillment of the Hallel psalms (Pss 113–118) that structured Jewish festival worship, particularly Passover. Just as the Hallel celebrated God's past deliverance from Egypt, the heavenly Hallelujah celebrates the final deliverance from Babylon. The progression from Babylon's judgment (ch. 18) to the Hallelujah chorus (19:1–6) mirrors the Exodus narrative sequence: plague cycle → deliverance → praise (Exod 7–15). The 'smoke rising forever' (v. 3) draws on Isaiah 34:10 (Edom's perpetual desolation) and functions as a permanent memorial to God's justice—not as vindictive excess but as the eternal evidence that evil has been decisively overcome.

Osborne structures the passage as a heavenly liturgy with four movements: the multitude's praise (vv. 1–2), the response affirming permanence (v. 3), the elders' and creatures' confirmation (v. 4), and the throne-voice summoning universal praise (v. 5). This liturgical structure mirrors early Christian worship patterns and suggests that John is presenting eschatological reality in the form most familiar to the churches—corporate worship. The theological content moves from justice (vv. 1–3: God's judgment is true and just) to doxology (vv. 4–5: praise from every rank and station), establishing that the proper response to divine justice is worship, not debate.

hist.context: The four 'Hallelujah' cries of Revelation 19:1–6 are the only occurrences of this word in the New Testament, making this passage the eschatological Hallel. The first two (vv. 1, 3) respond to Babylon's destruction, celebrating God's true and just judgments and the permanence of her ruin ('her smoke goes up for ever and ever'). The third (v. 4) comes from the twenty-four elders and the four living creatures, connecting this scene to the throne-room worship of chapters 4–5. The fourth (v. 6) inaugurates the marriage supper announcement. The entire sequence answers the call of 18:20 to 'Rejoice over her, you heavens!' and fulfills the martyrs' plea of 6:9–11 for divine vindication. The theological movement is from judgment (Babylon's fall) to celebration (the Lamb's marriage)—the two are inseparable because the removal of the corrupt system makes room for the consummation of the covenant relationship between Christ and His church.

sections.header: Verses 6-10 — "The Marriage Supper of the Lamb"

heb.paragraph: The noun gamos (wedding, marriage feast) appears in the singular with the genitive tou arniou (of the Lamb), presenting Christ as the bridegroom. The Old Testament precedent is the covenant-marriage metaphor of Hosea 2:16–20, Isaiah 54:5–8, and Ezekiel 16:8–14, where God is Israel's husband. The bridal imagery also echoes the Song of Solomon read allegorically. In the New Testament, Jesus employed wedding imagery in his parables (Matt 22:1–14; 25:1–13), and Paul described the church as Christ's bride (2 Cor 11:2; Eph 5:25–32). The Lamb's wedding is the consummation of this entire biblical trajectory—the covenant relationship between God and His people reaches its eschatological fulfillment.

refs.note: Paul describes the husband-wife relationship as a mystery that 'refers to Christ and the church.' The marriage supper of the Lamb (Rev 19:7–9) is the eschatological fulfillment of this Pauline mystery: the union between Christ and His church, anticipated in every earthly marriage, reaches its consummation in the new creation.

Jesus' parable of the wedding banquet depicts a king who invites guests to his son's wedding feast. Those who refuse are judged, and a guest without a wedding garment is expelled. Revelation 19:7–9 presents the reality behind the parable: the Lamb's wedding has arrived, His bride has made herself ready, and the wedding garment—fine linen representing righteous acts—is given to her.

Isaiah rejoices: 'He has clothed me with garments of salvation and arrayed me in a robe of his righteousness, as a bridegroom adorns his head like a priest, and as a bride adorns herself with her jewels.' The bride's fine linen in Revelation 19:8 fulfills this Isaianic promise of eschatological adornment, where salvation and righteousness are the garments of the redeemed.

notes.note: The fourth Hallelujah introduces the marriage supper, transitioning from judgment to celebration. 'Our Lord God Almighty reigns' (v. 6) is the definitive declaration of God's sovereign kingship, now uncontested. The Lamb's wedding has come—this is the moment the entire redemptive plan has been moving toward. The bride has 'made herself ready,' indicating active preparation through faithful obedience during the tribulation.

The bride's fine linen is identified as 'the righteous acts of God's holy people.' This is not the imputed righteousness of justification (which is Christ's alone) but the practical righteousness of sanctification—the obedient life lived by the Spirit's power. Both are necessary: imputed righteousness grants standing; lived righteousness provides the wedding garment.

The beatitude 'Blessed are those who are invited to the wedding supper of the Lamb' distinguishes between the bride (the church) and the guests (Old Testament saints and tribulation believers). The wedding supper is a literal, eschatological feast that will inaugurate the millennial kingdom.

John's impulse to worship the angel and the angel's sharp correction establishes the absolute exclusivity of divine worship. Even in the ecstasy of prophetic revelation, worship belongs to God alone. The statement that 'the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy' means that all true prophecy points to Christ and bears witness to Him—Jesus is the content and goal of all prophetic speech.

The marriage metaphor reveals that God's ultimate purpose is not merely to save sinners from judgment but to unite them to Himself in covenant love. Calvin would stress that this union is entirely of grace: the bride 'made herself ready,' but the fine linen 'was given to her' (edothē, divine passive). Even the righteous acts of the saints are gifts of grace—the bride prepares, but God provides the means of preparation. This dialectic of human responsibility and divine enablement is the heart of Reformed sanctification.

The angel's refusal of worship is a standing rebuke to all idolatry—including the veneration of angels, saints, and religious authorities. Calvin was particularly insistent on this point: no creature, however exalted, may receive the worship that belongs to God alone. The identification of Jesus' testimony with the spirit of prophecy means that all legitimate prophetic utterance is Christocentric—prophecy that does not point to Christ is, by definition, false prophecy.

The aorist ēlthen (has come) followed by the aorist hētoimasen (has prepared) presents both the wedding and the preparation as completed actions from a prophetic standpoint. The verb hētoimasen heautēn (she has prepared herself) is grammatically active/reflexive, indicating the bride's own agency in preparation, though v. 8 adds edothē autē (it was given to her), balancing human preparation with divine provision.

The phrase hē gar martyria Iēsou estin to pneuma tēs prophēteias (for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy) is grammatically ambiguous: 'the testimony of Jesus' could be either a subjective genitive (Jesus' own testimony) or an objective genitive (testimony about Jesus). Both readings are theologically valid, and the ambiguity may be intentional—prophecy is both from Jesus and about Jesus.

Beale reads the marriage supper as the eschatological fulfillment of the covenant formula 'I will be your God, and you will be my people' (Lev 26:12; Jer 31:33; Rev 21:3). The entire biblical narrative moves toward this consummation: creation established the context, the Fall disrupted it, redemption restored it, and the Lamb's wedding consummates it. The fine linen represents not individual moral achievements but the collective faithful witness of the covenant community throughout history—the 'righteous acts of the saints' are the accumulated testimonies of those who conquered the beast 'by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony' (12:11).

Osborne identifies this passage as the structural counterpart to the prostitute's introduction in 17:1–6: as the prostitute was adorned in purple, scarlet, gold, and precious stones (17:4), the bride is clothed in fine linen, bright and clean (19:8). The contrast is between corrupted luxury and pure righteousness, between seductive adornment and holy beauty. The marriage supper functions theologically as the positive counterpart to Babylon's fall: God's purpose is not merely to destroy the corrupt but to unite the faithful to Himself. The angel's refusal of worship (v. 10) guards against a persistent temptation in both ancient and modern religion—the deification of the messenger rather than worship of the God who sends him.

hist.context: The announcement of the Lamb's marriage supper (vv. 6–9) represents the climactic consummation of the covenant relationship between Christ and His people—the theological counterpart to Babylon's destruction. As the prostitute represented the false, adulterous relationship between humanity and the world system, the bride represents the true, faithful relationship between Christ and the church. The bride's fine linen (v. 8) is explicitly interpreted: it represents 'the righteous acts of God's holy people,' indicating that the wedding garment is not imputed righteousness alone but the lived-out faithfulness of the saints. The beatitude of verse 9—'Blessed are those who are invited to the wedding supper of the Lamb'—is the fourth of Revelation's seven beatitudes. John's attempt to worship the angel (v. 10) and the angel's refusal ('I am a fellow servant') establishes a critical principle: only God is to be worshipped. The angel identifies 'the testimony of Jesus' as 'the spirit of prophecy,' linking Christological witness with prophetic utterance.

heb.paragraph: The dual title pistos kai alēthinos (faithful and true) was first applied to Christ in the letter to the Laodicean church (3:14). Its reappearance here at the parousia creates an inclusio spanning the entire book: the Christ who addressed the churches in chapters 2–3 is the same Christ who now rides forth in judgment. The adjective pistos (faithful, trustworthy) emphasizes reliability—Christ fulfills every promise. The adjective alēthinos (true, genuine) emphasizes ontological reality—Christ is the true King in contrast to the beast's counterfeit kingdom. Together, the pair affirms that Christ's return is both the vindication of His promises and the revelation of ultimate reality.

heb.gloss: King of kings and Lord of lords
heb.paragraph: This superlative title combines two Old Testament supremacy formulas: 'King of kings' (Ezek 26:7 of Nebuchadnezzar; Dan 2:37 of the same) and 'Lord of lords' (Deut 10:17; Ps 136:3 of Yahweh). By combining and applying both to Christ, John makes an unambiguous claim of absolute divine sovereignty. The title is inscribed 'on his robe and on his thigh' (v. 16)—either on the part of his robe covering his thigh (a warrior's riding position) or as a double inscription emphasizing visibility. This is the ultimate Christological title in Revelation, surpassing all earthly claims to authority.

refs.note: Isaiah's vision of the warrior from Edom with garments stained crimson from the winepress provides the primary Old Testament background for Christ's blood-dipped robe (Rev 19:13) and the winepress of God's fury (Rev 19:15). In Isaiah, God treads the winepress alone because no one could help; in Revelation, Christ conquers alone because no one else is needed—His word is sufficient.

Daniel's vision of 'one like a son of man' who receives authority, glory, and sovereign power from the Ancient of Days reaches its fulfillment in the rider on the white horse. The many crowns (diademata polla, Rev 19:12) correspond to the universal dominion given to the Son of Man: 'all nations and peoples of every language worshiped him. His dominion is an everlasting dominion.'

The title 'the Word of God' (ho logos tou theou, Rev 19:13) connects Revelation's warrior Christology with the Johannine prologue's incarnation theology. The Word who 'was with God and was God' and who 'became flesh and made his dwelling among us' now rides forth in power to judge and make war. The same Word who created all things (John 1:3) now conquers all opposition.

notes.note: This is the Second Coming of Jesus Christ—the event toward which all of Revelation has been moving. He rides a white horse, symbolizing conquest and triumph. His eyes of blazing fire indicate omniscient, penetrating judgment. The many crowns (diadems, not victors' wreaths) represent His universal sovereignty. His robe dipped in blood is not His own blood shed at Calvary but the blood of His enemies, fulfilling Isaiah 63:1–6. He is called 'the Word of God'—the same Logos of John 1:1, now revealed as the divine warrior.

The armies of heaven—angels and glorified saints—follow Christ on white horses, dressed in fine linen. Notably, they carry no weapons. The battle is Christ's alone; His followers are witnesses, not combatants. The church accompanies the King but does not fight the King's battle—His word is sufficient.

The sharp sword from Christ's mouth is His word—the spoken decree of divine judgment. He will 'rule them with an iron scepter' (Ps 2:9) and 'tread the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty' (Isa 63:3). The title 'KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS' inscribed on His robe declares His absolute supremacy over every earthly and cosmic power. No competing claim to authority can stand.

The angel's invitation to the birds for the 'great supper of God' (v. 17) is a grim counterpart to the 'wedding supper of the Lamb' (v. 9). There are only two suppers in the eschaton: you are either a guest at the wedding or a course at the carnage. This echoes Ezekiel 39:17–20, where God invites birds and beasts to feast on the slain at the defeat of Gog.

The beast and the false prophet are captured and thrown alive into the lake of fire—the first occupants of this eternal destination. There is no battle narrative; Christ simply defeats them by the word of His mouth. The kings and their armies are killed, and the birds gorge on their flesh. The total absence of combat description is theologically significant: Christ's victory is not a contest but a decree. Evil is not overcome by superior force but by sovereign authority.

Calvin would see in the rider on the white horse the ultimate vindication of Christ's kingship, which the world has contested since His first advent. The blood-dipped robe is not passive suffering but active judgment—the cross was where Christ bore the world's sin; the parousia is where He executes the world's sentence. The sword from His mouth confirms that Christ's weapon is His word: He created by speaking (Gen 1), He sustains by His powerful word (Heb 1:3), and He judges by the same word. The title 'King of kings' is the final answer to every earthly tyrant and every competing claim to ultimate authority.

The anticlimactic nature of the battle is profoundly theological: evil is not God's equal, not even His serious rival. The beast and false prophet are simply 'captured'—no Homeric combat, no struggle. Calvin would insist that this reveals the fundamental asymmetry between Creator and creature, between sovereign God and rebellious creation. The nations rage (Ps 2:1), but God laughs (Ps 2:4). The lake of fire is not God's last resort but His predetermined destination for all rebellion.

The clause echōn onoma gegrammenon ho oudeis oiden ei mē autos (having a name written that no one knows except himself) raises the question of why the rider has an unknowable name alongside known names (Faithful and True, Word of God, King of kings). The unknowable name likely reflects the Old Testament tradition that the divine name contains mystery beyond full human comprehension (Judg 13:18; Isa 9:6 where pele', 'wonderful,' implies transcendent mystery).

The phrase bebammenon haimati (dipped in blood) presents a textual variant: some manuscripts read rerantismenon (sprinkled). Either reading evokes Isaiah 63:1–3, where the warrior's garments are stained with the blood of the enemies he has trodden in the winepress. The perfect participle indicates a prior action with present results—the blood is already on the robe before the battle of vv. 19–21, suggesting the blood is from the cross or from prior judgments, not from the imminent conflict.

Beale reads the rider on the white horse as the fulfillment of the divine warrior tradition that spans from the Exodus (Exod 15:3), through the prophets (Isa 42:13; 59:16–18; 63:1–6), to the Psalms (Ps 2; 45; 110). The blood on the robe precedes the battle, aligning with Isaiah 63's winepress imagery where God has already trampled the nations. The title 'Word of God' connects the martial christophany with creation theology: the Word that spoke the cosmos into existence now speaks judgment upon the rebellious cosmos. The sharp sword from his mouth is not a metaphor for military force but a literal depiction of the efficacy of divine speech—God's word accomplishes what it declares (Isa 55:10–11), whether in creation, redemption, or judgment.

The absence of actual combat is the theological crux of the passage. Beale argues that this is deliberate: the 'battle' of Armageddon (16:16) turns out to be no battle at all. The beast and his allies are simply defeated by the word of Christ. This subverts all militaristic expectations and reveals that God's power operates not through superior violence but through sovereign decree. The lake of fire receiving the beast and false prophet represents the inauguration of the final state of judgment—these are the first occupants of the destination that will ultimately receive all who resist God (20:14–15).

Osborne identifies four christological titles in the passage—Faithful and True (v. 11), a hidden name (v. 12), the Word of God (v. 13), and King of kings and Lord of lords (v. 16)—each revealing a different dimension of Christ's person and work. The accumulation of titles creates a comprehensive Christology: Christ is relationally faithful, transcendently mysterious, cosmically creative, and politically supreme. The white horse, blazing eyes, blood-stained robe, and many crowns combine prophetic, priestly, and royal imagery into a single overwhelming figure that transcends any single Old Testament category.

Osborne notes the structural parallel between the two 'suppers': the wedding supper of the Lamb (v. 9) and the great supper of God (v. 17). These represent the only two eschatological destinies—feast with Christ or become carrion for the birds. The battle's anticlimactic resolution (capture without combat) confirms that the sword from Christ's mouth is His word of judgment, not a literal weapon. The theological point is that Christ conquers by decree, not by struggle—His sovereignty is not contested but absolute.

hist.context: The vision of Christ on the white horse (vv. 11–21) is the climactic christophany of Revelation and the most detailed depiction of the Second Coming in the New Testament. Christ appears as a divine warrior, riding a white horse (cf. 6:2, where the first horseman's identity is debated), with eyes like blazing fire (cf. 1:14; 2:18), many crowns (contrasting the dragon's seven crowns, 12:3, and the beast's ten, 13:1), a name known only to himself (v. 12), and a robe dipped in blood (v. 13)—evoking the blood-stained warrior of Isaiah 63:1–6 who treads the winepress of divine wrath. The title 'the Word of God' (ho logos tou theou, v. 13) uniquely connects Revelation's Christology with the Johannine prologue (John 1:1, 14). The armies of heaven follow on white horses (v. 14), but Christ alone wields the weapon: a sharp sword from his mouth (v. 15), symbolizing the conquering power of His word. The battle itself is anticlimactic—there is no actual combat described. The beast and false prophet are simply 'captured' and thrown into the lake of fire (v. 20), and the rest are killed by the sword from Christ's mouth (v. 21). The victory is total, effortless, and exclusively Christ's.

themes.note: Revelation 19 is the theological climax of the book, uniting worship and warfare in a single vision. The fourfold Hallelujah establishes that divine judgment is a proper object of worship. The Lamb's marriage consummates the covenant relationship. Christ's effortless victory reveals that sovereignty is not contested but absolute.

rows.label: vv.1-3
rows.text: First and second Hallelujahs: celebrating Babylon's judgment

rows.label: v.4
rows.text: Third Hallelujah: elders and living creatures confirm

rows.label: v.5
rows.text: Throne voice: call to universal praise

rows.label: vv.6-8
rows.text: Fourth Hallelujah: marriage of the Lamb announced

rows.label: v.9
rows.text: Fourth beatitude: blessed are the invited

rows.label: v.10
rows.text: Angel refuses worship; testimony of Jesus = spirit of prophecy

rows.label: vv.11-16
rows.text: Christ the divine warrior: four titles, robe of blood, sharp sword

rows.label: vv.17-18
rows.text: Great supper of God: angel summons the birds

rows.label: vv.19-21
rows.text: Beast and false prophet captured; armies slain by Christ's word

lit.note: Revelation 19 divides into two major movements: heavenly celebration (vv. 1–10) and divine conquest (vv. 11–21). The transition at verse 11 ('I saw heaven standing open') marks a shift from liturgy to warfare. The two 'suppers'—the Lamb's wedding feast (v. 9) and God's carrion feast (vv. 17–18)—frame the ultimate binary choice of eschatological destiny.

hebtext.note: Hallēlouia is a Greek transliteration of Hebrew hallĕlû Yāh (praise Yah[weh]). Remarkably, this is the only occurrence of the word in the entire New Testament, yet it appears four times in this chapter (vv. 1, 3, 4, 6). The term is deeply rooted in the Psalter, where it opens and closes the Hallel psalms (Pss 113–118, 146–150). The noun gamos (wedding, marriage feast) appears in the singular with the genitive tou arniou (of the Lamb), presenting Christ as the bridegroom. The Old Testament precedent is the covenant-marriage metaphor of Hosea 2:16–20, Isaiah 54:5–8, and Ezekiel 16:8–14, where God is Israel's husband. The bridal imagery also echoes the Song of Solomon read allegorically. In the New Testament, Jesus employed wedding imagery in his parables (Matt 22:1–14; 25:1–13), and Paul described the church as Christ's bride (2 Cor 11:2; Eph 5:25–32). The Lamb's wedding is the consummation of this entire biblical trajectory—the covenant relationship between God and His people reaches its eschatological fulfillment.

The dual title pistos kai alēthinos (faithful and true) was first applied to Christ in the letter to the Laodicean church (3:14). Its reappearance here at the parousia creates an inclusio spanning the entire book: the Christ who addressed the churches in chapters 2–3 is the same Christ who now rides forth in judgment. The adjective pistos (faithful, trustworthy) emphasizes reliability—Christ fulfills every promise. The adjective alēthinos (true, genuine) emphasizes ontological reality—Christ is the true King in contrast to the beast's counterfeit kingdom. Together, the pair affirms that Christ's return is both the vindication of His promises and the revelation of ultimate reality.

hebtext.gloss: King of kings and Lord of lords
hebtext.note: This superlative title combines two Old Testament supremacy formulas: 'King of kings' (Ezek 26:7 of Nebuchadnezzar; Dan 2:37 of the same) and 'Lord of lords' (Deut 10:17; Ps 136:3 of Yahweh). By combining and applying both to Christ, John makes an unambiguous claim of absolute divine sovereignty. The title is inscribed 'on his robe and on his thigh' (v. 16)—either on the part of his robe covering his thigh (a warrior's riding position) or as a double inscription emphasizing visibility. This is the ultimate Christological title in Revelation, surpassing all earthly claims to authority.

Reads The Rider on the White Horse within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
